content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

July 14, 1975

Ian Schlesser
Director, Public Affairs
KPIX 5
San Francisco 94103

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-147

Dear Mr. Schlesser:

Thank you for your letter of June 27, 1975, requesting the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an opinion regarding the reporting duties of KPIX under the Political Reform Act.

Your request for a formal opinion is denied because the issues you have presented raise no substantial questions of interpretation of the Political Reform Act.

Expenditures incurred by KPIX are not chargeable to proponents or opponents of an initiative if both sides are represented on the program.

The issue raised in your third question, regarding whether expenditures incurred by persons appearing on the program are shareabe expenditures of proponents of opponents of the initiative, has been noticed for consideration at the August 6-7 meeting of the Commission. You are encouraged to present your views at that meeting.

Although no formal opinion will be issued, I hope the foregoing is sufficiently helpful. If you have any questions, I urge you to contact a member of the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the
Commission

DHL:jo

KPIX 5

2655 VAN NESS SAN FRANCISCO CALIFORNIA 94109 775-5123

Request No. 75-1157
Data Received 7-3-75
Response Due 9/17/75
GROUP
W

WESTINGHOUSE BROADCASTING COMPANY INC

June 27, 1975

Mr. Dan Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
926 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

As per my telephone conversation with Robert Stearns, I
am requesting an opinion under Section 83114 on the following
questions:

Whether any expenditure incurred by KPIX
in the course of presenting a Public Affairs
debate on Nuclear Power would be chargeable
to the parties or participants - both sides
of the Initiative - for and against - will be
represented on the program?

Is the answer any different if the Initiative
on Nuclear Power is brought up and/or discussed?

Whether any expenditure incurred by a person
appearing on the program will be chargeable to
either side's limitations; such as salary,
or travel expenses - in or out of State?

I should explain that this special Public Affairs Program
is scheduled to be recorded July 20th for broadcast Wednesday,
July 30, 1975, 10:00-11:00PM as part of the Station's Impact
Project on Nuclear Power Week (July 26 - 30th). The question
posed for the debate is "Should there be a moratorium on
further construction of nuclear power plants?" Each side
will present witnesses for direct questioning and cross -
examination in a courtroom trial debate format. A 12-person
jury will deliberate and present a verdict.

-2-

Both sides are very cooperative with our effort to
make the public aware of this important issue.
However, they are concerned about how it will effect the
parties and participants as to the state's Fair Political
Practices Act.

I would appreciate an answer to the above questions
as soon as possible before our recording date.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Len Schlosser
Director/Public Affairs

LS:vp

Data received 7-9-75
Response Due 8/19/75

THELEN, MARRIN, JOHNSON & BRIDGES
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
TWO EMBARCADERO CENTER
SAN FRANCISCO 94111
(415) 392-8322
CABLE THEMAR 76-34-0906

July 1, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
928 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Ms. Natalie West

Re: Opinion Request Pursuant to §83114,
    Fair Political Practices Act

Dear Ms. West:

    In connection with potential activities in the future
by one of our clients, we request that the Commission issue an
opinion on the following question:

    "Must a corporation incurring expenditures in
communicating with its own employees regarding
a statewide ballot measure (and therefore quali-
fying as a "committee" pursuant to §82013 of
the Act) file a §85302 'statement of intent' if
such expenditures will exceed $10,000?"

    Thank you for any consideration which the Commission
may give to this request.

                                        Sincerely,

                        THELEN, MARRIN, JOHNSON & BRIDGES


                        Joseph A. Darrell

sections.question: Whether any expenditure incurred by KPIX in the course of presenting a Public Affairs debate on Nuclear Power would be chargeable to the parties or participants - both sides of the Initiative - for and against - will be represented on the program? Is the answer any different if the Initiative on Nuclear Power is brought up and/or discussed? Whether any expenditure incurred by a person appearing on the program will be chargeable to either side's limitations; such as salary, or travel expenses - in or out of State?
sections.conclusion: Expenditures incurred by KPIX are not chargeable to proponents or opponents of an initiative if both sides are represented on the program. The issue raised in your third question, regarding whether expenditures incurred by persons appearing on the program are chargeable to the proponents or opponents of the initiative, has been noticed for consideration at the August 6-7 meeting of the Commission.
sections.question_synthetic: A television station seeks clarification on whether expenditures incurred in presenting a balanced public affairs debate program on a nuclear power initiative would be chargeable to proponents or opponents under the Political Reform Act, and whether costs incurred by individual participants in the debate would count against campaign expenditure limitations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion, finding no substantial questions of interpretation requiring formal resolution. However, the Commission provided limited guidance that a broadcaster's expenditures for a balanced debate program are not chargeable to either side if both sides are represented, while deferring the question of participants' individual costs to a future Commission meeting.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (June 27, 1975) and the FPPC response (July 14, 1975). A third unrelated opinion request (July 1, 1975) from Thelen Marrin law firm is included in the document text but does not appear to be responsive to the primary transaction. The FPPC response explicitly denies the request for a formal opinion while providing substantive guidance.

QUESTION: Whether any expenditure incurred by KPIX in the course of presenting a Public Affairs debate on Nuclear Power would be chargeable to the parties or participants - both sides of the Initiative - for and against - will be represented on the program? Is the answer any different if the Initiative on Nuclear Power is brought up and/or discussed? Whether any expenditure incurred by a person appearing on the program will be chargeable to either side's limitations; such as salary, or travel expenses - in or out of State?

CONCLUSION: Expenditures incurred by KPIX are not chargeable to proponents or opponents of an initiative if both sides are represented on the program. The issue raised in your third question, regarding whether expenditures incurred by persons appearing on the program are chargeable to the proponents or opponents of the initiative, has been noticed for consideration at the August 6-7 meeting of the Commission.

embedding.summary: KPIX-TV requested an FPPC opinion regarding whether expenditures for a balanced public affairs debate program on nuclear power initiative issues would be attributable to proponents or opponents. The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but indicated that broadcaster expenditures are not chargeable if both sides are represented, while deferring the subsidiary question about individual participants' costs to an upcoming Commission meeting.
